chunks.text: Research is the foundation of Berkeley EECS Faculty and students work together on cutting-edge projects that cross disciplinary boundaries to improve everyday life and make a difference. Our leadership in research is the key to maintaining a vibrant curriculum that prepares our students to become leaders in their chosen fields.
chunks.question: What is the role of research in Berkeley EECS?
chunks.answer: Foundation for curriculum and leadership development.

chunks.text: EECS researchers join together to explore a broad range of areas and ideas, from the tiniest nano particle to the latest Artificial Intelligence.
chunks.question: What range of topics do EECS researchers explore?

chunks.text: Our Researchers delve deeply into a wide variety of subjects, from the Chisel language for advanced hardware design to wearable sensor arrays for personalized medicine.
chunks.question: What are examples of EECS research subjects?
chunks.answer: Chisel language and wearable sensor arrays.

chunks.text: The weekly EECS Colloquium offers our faculty a chance to invite speakers offering a wide range of experiences covering topics in both Electrical Engineering and Computer Science to engage with the department.
chunks.question: What is the purpose of the EECS Colloquium?
chunks.answer: Invite speakers on EE and CS topics.

chunks.text: Research allows you to pursue your interests, to learn something new, to hone your problem-solving skills and to challenge yourself in new ways. Working on a faculty-initiated research project gives you the opportunity work closely with a mentor–a faculty member or other experienced researcher. With a self-initiated research project, you leave Berkeley with a product that represents the distillation of your interests and studies and, possibly, a real contribution to knowledge.
chunks.question: What are the benefits of undergraduate research?
chunks.answer: Develop skills and contribute to knowledge.
